title: Step 2 Ok([]) detail message is misleading when blockResult is Err
spec_quote: REQ-018 AC: 'CorruptedFile.detail is set to a human-readable summary of the unexpected error.' (block-parse failures should similarly carry a useful detail string for diagnostic traceability — REQ-017 says BlockParseError variants carry `line` and `detail` that should surface).
code_quote: scan-vault.ts:153-163 — `if (!blockResult.ok || blockResult.value.length === 0) { const detail = blockResult.ok ? "empty block array" : blockResultDetail(blockResult.error); corruptedFiles.push({ filePath, failure: { kind: "hydrate", reason: "block-parse" }, detail, }); continue; }`
issue: The condition `!blockResult.ok || blockResult.value.length === 0` correctly captures both failure modes, but the resulting `detail` field uses the literal string `'empty block array'` for the Ok([]) case. There is no test asserting the value of `corrupted.detail` for either branch (step2-block-parse.test.ts checks reason but never detail), so a regression that swaps the two branches (e.g., always producing 'empty block array') would go unnoticed. Additionally, the BlockParseError variants `unterminated-code-fence` and `malformed-structure` carry useful line/detail metadata (parse-markdown-to-blocks.ts:36-38) that blockResultDetail concatenates correctly — but again, no test asserts the format. Diagnostic traceability is therefore not verified.
recommended_remediation: Add Tier-2 tests in step2-block-parse.test.ts that assert `corrupted.detail` matches expected substrings: `'unterminated code fence at line 3'` for the unterminated-code-fence stub, `'malformed structure at line 5: ...'` for malformed-structure, and `'empty block array'` for the Ok([]) stub. This locks in the diagnostic contract and prevents silent regression.
